narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 21. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on past students' academic and background data, estimated an 82.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this model to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. The three biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class—had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it signals consistently lower performance compared to peers who passed the bar. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern, showing early academic struggles that stood out against the average student's mid-range standing. Your LSAT score of 21, well below the dataset average of about 37, added further weight toward the failure prediction, as higher scores are strongly linked to bar success in the model's view. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 was somewhat below average and your full-time status is common among admitted students, these played smaller roles here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your rankings and test score placed you in the lower tail of the distribution, tipping the overall balance against admission. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal qualities, but rather how these metrics aligned with historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that better match successful profiles. Many students in similar situations have improved their standings and gained admission elsewhere—persistence and targeted preparation can shift the odds in your favor.
